terms.short: Your personal copy of someone else’s repository on GitHub.
terms.definition: A fork is your own copy of a project. You can change it freely, then open a pull request to suggest those changes back to the original project.

terms.short: The original project you forked from.
terms.definition: Upstream usually means the main/original repository. After you fork, you pull updates from upstream so your copy stays current.

terms.short: The default name for your remote repository on GitHub.
terms.definition: Origin is just a nickname Git uses for a remote URL—usually your fork or the repo you cloned. It is not a special Git command.

terms.short: Download a repository to your computer.
terms.definition: Cloning copies a remote Git repo (including history) onto your machine so you can work locally.

terms.short: A saved snapshot of your changes with a message.
terms.definition: A commit records a set of staged changes plus a message. Think of it as a checkpoint you can return to later.

terms.short: A separate line of work in Git.
terms.definition: Branches let you try changes without breaking the main line of the project. You usually create a branch for each bugfix or feature.

terms.short: Replay your commits on top of another branch.
terms.definition: Rebasing moves your commits onto a newer base (often main). It can keep history cleaner, but you should avoid rebasing shared commits until you know the risks.

terms.short: When Git can’t auto-combine two sets of edits.
terms.definition: A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same lines. You manually choose the correct final text, then mark the conflict resolved.

terms.short: A proposal to merge your changes into a project.
terms.definition: A pull request (PR) shows your commits, lets others review them, and—if approved—merges your branch into the target branch (often main).

terms.short: A tracked bug report, idea, or task on GitHub.
terms.definition: Issues are discussion tickets. They help maintainers and contributors track bugs, features, and questions in one place.

terms.short: People who review and steward the project.
terms.definition: Maintainers decide what gets merged, keep the project healthy, and guide contributors. Be patient and respectful when waiting for review.

terms.short: Anyone who helps a project (code, docs, design, triage).
terms.definition: Contributors improve a project by submitting PRs, fixing docs, reporting bugs, or helping others. You don’t need to be an expert to contribute.

terms.short: “Looks Good To Me” — a friendly approval shorthand.
terms.definition: Reviewers often write LGTM when a change looks ready to merge. It is informal approval, not a special GitHub button by itself.

terms.short: A beginner-friendly task labeled for new contributors.
terms.definition: Projects mark some issues as good first issues so newcomers can start with smaller, well-scoped tasks.

terms.short: When someone checks your PR before it merges.
terms.definition: Code review is feedback on your changes—correctness, style, tests, and clarity. Treat comments as collaboration, not personal criticism.

terms.short: Automated checks that run on your PR.
terms.definition: Continuous Integration (CI) runs tests, linting, and builds automatically. A red CI check usually means something needs fixing before merge.

terms.short: A Git nickname for a repo hosted elsewhere (like GitHub).
terms.definition: A remote is a pointer to another copy of the repository, often on GitHub. Common remotes are origin (your copy) and upstream (the original).

terms.short: The holding area for files you plan to commit next.
terms.definition: Staging (`git add`) selects which changes go into the next commit. Unstaged edits stay in your working files until you add them.

terms.short: The default primary branch in many projects.
terms.definition: Main (sometimes still called master) is usually the stable default branch. Feature work typically happens on other branches, then merges into main.

terms.short: A human-readable list of what changed between versions.
terms.definition: A changelog summarizes features, fixes, and breaking changes so users and contributors can quickly see what is new.
